LOUVAIN AND ITS IRISH PRESS.
By R. J. KeLny, K.C.

The dlustrious prelate Florence Conry, or Mac Conry, who

| f_o_unded the College of 8t. Anthony of Padua at Louvain, and set up
‘the printing press there, deserves special mention in your pages

The
site of this College, which wvas, up #ill recently, devoted to the train-
ing of boys as a kind of industrial school, has been mow demolished

~ by the ‘‘ uniformed savages '’ who are so wuthlessly ravaging. the fair

land of Belgium. I wisited it some four years ago, and saw there a

- mural tablet erected to commemorate the mame and services of the

founder, I'lorence Mac Conroy, to whom Ireland as swell as Belgium owes
go much. He was Archbishop of Tuam in 1609, and was borm iny Con-
naught, either at Galway or, as some think, in the Co. Letrim in 1560.
He went to Louvain and was educated there, and subsequently to Spain.
He was a man of great eminence and learning, and particularly noted
tor his studies of the works of St. Augustine. He returned to Ireland

~ In 1584, and was received into the Framciscan order at their famous
. 8ohool at Moyne, near Ballina.

He was appointed in 1588 Prowincial
of the Irish Franciscans. He sailed from Spain for Ireland with the
Atmada, and was in one of the ships ‘wrecked off the coast of Scot-
land, He had therefore to return to Slpai:n. In 1593 he tramslated
from the Spanish a work entitled ‘‘ Christian Instruction.’ ) He did it

B a5 ho- says in the Preface, ‘‘ eut-of charity to the souls of the Gael,”’

and hé' adds that he had no opportunity of sending it to Ireland until

1598, “He wrote am account of ' his shipwreck in a work - entitled

¢« Traveller from Jerusalem to
Florence Mac Conroy was not

only a preat student, but a very accomplished diplomatist and states-
Man. His letters on publio affains at the time to. and from eminenb
men are remarkabl y able dnd far-seeing. He wrote from Valladolid in
1314 to one of the Catholic members of the Inish Parliament, .and
ﬂlﬁrem finds fault with their acquiesence in. the Act of Atbainder.
He signg the letter as Arrchbishop of Tuam. It was in consequence of

Peregrinus Iericonihus,” or the

the 'l‘equeﬁt of O’Neil that Flmenoe Mac Conroy was given the mitre

by the Pope, and appointed in March, 1609, Axrchbishop of Tuapy
He was consecrated in a church on the Salermian Road in Rome by
Cardinal Maffey, ‘‘ protector of Ireland.”” He obtaimned the consept
of the King of Spain, with whom he was on terms of the greates
friendship, to the foundation of the College of St. Anthony of Pady
at Louvain for the education of Irish youths, and in 1609 the founds.
tion was formally laid by the Archduke Albert. In 1618 Mac Conrgy

~ presented to the Council of Spain O’Sullivan Bere’s ‘' Relation of

Ireland,”’ and in 1619 his own account of the ‘‘ Severities practised by
the English Heretics towards the Irish Catholics.”” He was 20 years
Archbishop of Tuam. A life-sized portrait of him is frescoed on the
walls of St. Isidore’s Franciscan Comnvent in Rome, and is reproduced
in Fr. Mechan’s ‘‘ INate and Fortunes of O’Neill,”’ etc. His contem-
porary in Tuam, first Protestant archbishop, was William Daniel, who
was also a great linguist and scholar, and translated into Irish the
Scriptures—a book still extant. He also translated the Book of

Common Prayer, but his Bible is still to be found in use. Mao Conroy

established the printing press at Louvain. He got Irish type for it,
and there turned out several books im both Irish and English, which
were sent into Jreland and were extensively ocirculated here. The
Louvain press was the only means of obtaining native and Catholic
litera.trum that the Irish had in these days for the Dubln press was
in the hands of English and Dublin Castle agents and instruments.
Dr. Mac Conroy was such an enthusiast on this matter of printing
that to encourage the art he learmed to set mp type himself. To the
Louvain press and to its illustrious founder, Ireland was for centuries
indebted for the oclassic works then in wse and im cinculation. From it
were subsequently published the Historical Works of the Right Rev.
Nicholas French, Bishop of Ferns, and among them we find *‘The
Bleeding Iphegenia,”’ ‘‘ The Settlement and Sale of Ireland,” ‘Let-

ters,” ete., all of great histomical interest and value. Dr. Mac Conroy

daed atb - M'adrrld in 1629, aged 69.

duning the nineteenth century includes all T have heen able to ascer
tain, Beyond .the newspapers the output is certainly meagre and mos
markedly so between 1859 and 1877. There must have been many Pam-

TREASURES IN MARSH’S LIBRARY, DUBLIN.

The Rev. Newport J. D. White, D.D., Librarian of Marsh’s, gives §
the following list of some of the treasures contained in the Library
appended to his admirable short history in' the Library Association B

Record ’’ for March, 1899.

Of the MSS. the oldest perhaps is the * Codex Armachanus,’ ..97 T 1
fourteenth century volume of saints,.chiefly Irish. It ds - commonlys .
but mistakenly, called ‘‘ Cod. Kilkenniensis.”” The MS. to whioh tuhah B

Professor Stokes was continually finding interesting things in the
bbrary, e.g., a copy of an original indulgence issued by Cardinals 1
kit Wolsey and Campeggio, about 1520, for the purpose of raising fundg
;f_ - for the building of Hereford Cathedral. This was found pasted into 1

the coven of Pymsons Psalter mentioned above. I myself found ina |

 volume of old tracts a copy of the Ordinal of 1549, printed by Graftoen

in that year.”” Another interesting discovery was recently made by §

Mr. E. A. Phelps (formerly assistant Librarian) who, when examining i

a fifteenth century volume sent up from the Cashel Library, found two

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

At the Tea Table Talk on 6th January it was appropriate that the
' chair should be occupied by Mrs. Rentoul-Esler, the best-known of out
i Ulster women writers, whilst another lady from the same province
REE. entertained the gathening with a delightful paper. on ‘‘Am Ulstél‘ =
S ' Vﬂla,ge '  Miss Perry commenced her paper with a poetic desmptlon B
~ of Garvagh, the wvillage in question, from henr father’s fine poem  Foes
" and Friends.” She graphically described the scemes of her childhood, +
i - 1 , depicting the joys and sorrows of the people, their short and simple £
* annals, the legends that still survived in the place names, and ther. -
fairy lore. She touched off to the life the quaint characters 1?11*91"
impressed hen, and told many a witty anecdote, and story, racy of the
- poil, and illustrating the manners and customs of the villagers, thelr F
* - traditional lore and fierce party fights. She mentioned the mdfé'
e nntaﬂmle ;people who had spr 'ung from tﬂ]e di smmcst m-ll-known Wﬂ‘wte'm

.T L Bnanrbduﬂn (“G‘eirvms Guage”), Su.r J B Dworu;g‘harty, MP-, !
~ Andrew Onr, whose fine lyric, ‘‘The Sunny Sourb],l is thmsp
rbo be- foumd m fmwnw a.mﬂlwlognes, was a fneque-nrb visitor ab her fa'bheﬂ‘*‘ e 44

'

Dr. Croue, in moving a vote of thanks, said that Miss Perry, with
pa.rdanaible modesty, had omitted to mention amongst the Garvagh
'notwblmes the names of her father and brothers, so distinguished in the
scientific world. Nor would the tale be complete if no mention were
| pade of the Canning romance in real life. How the eldest son of the
{  pouse was disinherited, and, marrying a third-rate actress, died 1in

poverty, leaving @ boy omly three yeans old. That child so inauspici-

‘| ously born, lived to become the premier of England, who “ called the
| New Worid into exustemce to redress the balance of the Old’’! Mrr.
Elkin Mathews, the well-known publisher, seconded the vote, which was
capried with enthusiasm, conveyed by Mrs. Rentoul Esler, and suit-
Li'z . ably acknowledged. .
£ A similar function took place on 20th January, when Miss Emily
| Hickey presided over a full gathering. Miss Eva Gore Booth gave a
|| delightful meading from her fine dramatic poem, ‘‘The Triumph of
% Maeve,”’ which was listened to with rapt attention and frequently
'. - opplauded. The reader, by way of preface, acknow ledged that she had

taken libenties with the msually received versions of the legend, but
claimed that whilst treating historical subjects in poetic form, facts
| should be rigorously adhered to, yet myth or legends lent themselves
. to, and had always been treated with, a wide poetic license. She had
| placed her own constructions on the actions of the characters as they
appeared to her, from her studies of all the available sources of inform -
I: ation. Miss Hickey could not agree with the line taken by her fellow
postess, which placed her heroes, Fergus and Cuchullin, on an alto-
' . gethh,ar lower plane than any of the legends warranted, at the same
[ time bearing testimony to the poetic beauty of the piece and the
L charm of the interspersed lyrics. Other speakers followed.

AssoCIATION Books. "What booksellers call ¢ association books’’ are
frequently of much interest, if of little intrinsic value. They call to
mind recollections of their previous owners, of their lives and libraries,
and lead us sometimes to wonder into whose hands, oun own books, our
most cherished, possessions, may fall in the course of time, when Time
for us shall'be no more. Omne such, recently rescued from the penny
box, seemed to me to possess such interest, and I leave it to you to
decide whether you consider your readens might also be interested in
it. Itis ‘‘ Romulus and Remus: or Rome was mot built in a Day.
A olassical and, what one may call a most absurdly ridiculous
Burlesque, in one act. Being an attempt at Something Founded on
Roman History. By T. F. Dillon Croker. Printed for Private Circu-
lation, 1859.”” Performed. at the Theatre Royal, *‘‘ Donnybrook,”’
Blackheath. The author (1831-1912) was the only child of Crofton
Croker (1798-1854), ‘‘the nursling of the fairies,”’ as someone called
him, after the work by which he is best known—‘‘Inish- Fairy
Legends ”’—and the title page bears this inscription: ‘‘Robert Bell,
Esqr., with T. F. Dillon Croker’s Compliments.”” Robert Bell (1800-
1867) a Corkman, like Crofton Croker, has a whole page devoted to
him in the ‘¢ Dictiomary of National Biography,”” Vol. iv., which,
departing from its usual custom, says, ‘‘ his death was much and
widely regretted.’”’” He seems to have led a busy and prolific literary
life, and his annotated edition of the British Poets issued by his name-
sake, George Bell , Kingsway, is still reprinted. The book also bears

Anthony Trollope’s book plate, in the corner of which is wrutten

“R.B.,”” the initials of the former owner. How he came to possess

it is a pleasant little story meflecting credit on his head and hﬁawrb
It is told in Mr. Escott’s ‘ Anthony Trollope and His Friends.”

“ Robert, Bell , the once universally known book lover, critic a.nd
author, had left to his widow a smaller estate than was expected.
His library was anmounced for sale at Willis and Sotheby’s. ‘ This,’
said Trollope, ‘must not be. We all know the difference in value be-

‘tween buying and selling of books.” He at once saw the executors;
the auction arrangements were cancelled, and Trollope bought all "31.19‘ -

volumes at a price fixed by hims&]af, much above their market w

S Am, excellent pennyworth! We should be glad to print any Fithe-r it
| Notices of association books our readers may furnish us with.—Ed.

QUERIES AND REPLIES.

ForREMAN.—Are there any biographical details known of C. Fore-
man, author of *‘ Defence of the Courage, Honour and Leyalty of the
Irish,” of avhich an edition was edited by Thomas Davis? What were
the dates of the different editions? I understand that Foreman wrote
other pamphlets. Is a list of them available?

Cork. S. O°C.

# * Iirom a review of the work in ‘¢ The Nation’’ for 20th April,
1844, it appears that ‘‘ Charles FForman had served under Marlborough
in the low countries, and was a Protestant and an Englhishman. So
early as 1727 he wrote from Amsterdam a long letter addressed to
Sir Robert Sutton, urging the mecessity of requiring I‘rance as a
condition of amity to dismiss the Irish Brigade from. her service.
Over thirty years after he intervened in a controversy between the
Tory paper, ‘ The Craftsman,” and the whig organ, ‘ The Free Briton.’
The latter had. spoken with contempt of Irish courage. . . Forman,
then resident in Paris, veplied in the pamphlet here ufe-:-publ-ished. He
most, ably canvasses the statements of ‘The Free Briton’ as to the
wars in Ireland, and gives interesting and accuvate - notices of the
of the war in Ireland
it 1690-1.”

ten by Mr. O’Brien, from Cabirmoyle, Nowocastle West, and dated

Van der Maeren and his companion Mr. J ottrand—a, very able and
‘agreeable Belgian. The latter intends to write for publication his

observations upon Ireland and ats people.”” Could amy of your meaders
say whether M. Jottrand’s observations were published ? M. Conr
Wwas the son of an Irishman, who sought safety in Belgium after the
insuprection of 1798, or the attempt of Robert Emmet in 1803, and
a brother of the late distinguished engraver,

NAME oF Pray I'ounp. (Vol. vi.,, p. 104.) In the Egmont MSS,
(Hist. MSS. Commission), 1909 , at page 24 there is a letter from
Robert Bowyer where the date is given as 1670, and the play specified
as Ben Jonson’s ‘‘ Bartholomew Fair.”” Tradition, however, agrees
with ‘‘ The Chronological Remembrancer,”” Dublin, 1750, in assigning
the accident to 1671. W. J. LAWRENCE.

Dr. TrOMAS ANTISELL. (Vol. VI., pp. 80, 102.) This distinguished
chemist and geologist was born in Du-blm on Januwary 16th, 1817, and
was the son of Christopher Antisell and Margaret Daly, a King's Co.
lady. He was educated at a good middle-class school, and subsequently

passed through the College of Surgeons and Apothecaries Hall. After

taking his degree he became lecturer on Bobtany in the Peter Street
School of Medicine, and it was while engaged there that he identified
himself completely with the policy of the Young Irelanders, his most
intomate friend among them being, perhaps, Richard D’Alton Williams,
then a medical student. He published ‘‘ A Manual of Agricultural
Chemistry ’’ (1845), ‘Outlines of Irish Geology’ (1846), and “A
Report on the Sanitary Improvement of Dublin’’ (1847). After the

failure of the ’48 movement he emigrated to America and settled in _
Washington. He became very widely known as a geologist and chemist

by his papers in various American periodicals, and in 1854 was ap-

pointed by the U.S. Government State Geologist for California and

Arizona. During the Civil War he acted in a medical capacity in the

army, and in 1866 was appointed Chief Chemist of the U.S. Agricul-

tural Dept. In or about 1871 he was semt as one of a commission t0

Japan to report upon the resources of that empire, and he remained it |
the land of the ohrysamthemum for five years. On his return he be-
came a pwfemr at the Georgetown Medical College of Washington,
- D.C. He died in that capital on June 14th, 1893. He was maanrled;
;twace, firgt in 1841 to a Dublin lady named Nowlan, and seco:udly 111

119

THE [RISH BOOK LOVER.

1854 to a Detroit lady. He published in America several important
works and reponts on his special subjects. All through his life he was
dosely idemtified with various Inish-American societies and move-
ments, and 'was held in the highest esteem by all sections of American
opinion. |
D. J. O’D.

(Vol. VI., p. 80.) The * Letters of an Ims:h

were by W. H. Keating.

AUTHOR WANTED,
Catholic Layman ’

Enniscorthy. W. H. G. I.
FORTHCOMING WORKS. |

No life of the eccentric Earl of Bristol and Bishop of Derry, who
shared with Charlemont the leadership of the volunteers of 1782, and
built the ‘‘ Bishop’s Folly,”” has ever been written. Mr. W. S. Childe-
Pemberton, 12, Portman Street, London, W., a practised biographer,
intends to remedy this defect, and will be grateful for information
from private sources, letters, etc., to assist him in his task.

Messrs. Classell will publish in the spring ‘‘ Reminiscences and Let-
ters of Sir Robert Ball,”’ edited by his son, Mr. Valentine Ball.

Messrs. . W. O’Connell and R. M. Henry, of the Queen’s Uni-
versity, Belfast, have edited the ‘‘ Corpus Astronomise,’”’ a unique Irish
MS. in the possession of the Belfast Natural History and Philosophical
Society, being Manus O’Donnell’s seventeenth century version of
“The Lumarmio’> of Geronymo Cortes. Mr. David Nutt will publish
it shortly at 10s. 6d.

Messrs. Longmans announce that the usual issue of ‘‘ Hermathena,’

. which has been produced annually by the members of Trinity College,

Dublin, since 1873, will this year take the form: of an index number,

containing full references to the authorship and contents of all num-

bers from the beginming. After the publication of the Index *‘ Her-
mathena ’’ will resume its annual appearancse.

Mn, T. Werner Laourie has a book or the famous Waterford glass
coming out shortly. . It is by Mr. Sydney Lewis, who not only ranks
a3 an authonity on the subject, but has a fine collection of his own
from which many of the illustrations in the book wi Il be taken.

The posthumous book by the late Sister Nivedita, (Margaret Noble),

‘Which Messrs. Longmans will have ready next month. under the tatle

of “ Iootfalls of Tndian Hisbory,”’ deals with the reinterpretation of

the great ages of the past of India, especially in relation to the social

and religious consciousness of the people. Tt will be illustmated in

colour,
